content.problemStatement:
Check if the given binary tree is a valid Binary Search Tree (BST).

A valid BST adheres to the following criteria:


The left subtree of a node comprises only nodes with keys less than the node's key.
The right subtree of a node comprises only nodes with keys greater than the node's key.
Both the left and right subtrees must also be valid Binary Search Trees.
For eg., the given binary tree is a valid BST.



Function DeclarationFunction NameisBSTisBSTisBST – This function checks whether the given binary tree is a valid Binary Search Tree (BST).

Parameters

rootrootroot : A pointer to the root node of the binary tree




Each node is defined as:


`struct Node {
    int val;
    Node *left;
    Node *right;
};
`



Return Value

The function does **not return any value**.




It should **print**:



`"YES"` if the binary tree is a valid Binary Search Tree
`"NO"` otherwise


Constraints
1≤N≤100001 \leq N \leq 100001≤N≤10000
1≤pi,ci≤1000001 \leq p_i, c_i \leq 1000001≤pi​,ci​≤100000
Ri∈L,RR_i \in {L, R}Ri​∈L,R
All node values are **distinct**
The input represents a **valid binary tree structure**

Input Format


The first line contains a single integer NNN — the number of nodes in the binary tree.




The next N−1N - 1N−1 lines each contain three space-separated values:



pip_ipi​ — parent node value
cic_ici​ — child node value
RiR_iRi​ — either `L` or `R`, indicating whether the child is a left or right child




Output Format


Output a single line:



`"YES"` if the binary tree satisfies all Binary Search Tree properties
`"NO"` otherwise
constraints: The input describes a valid binary tree
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an integer N — the number of nodes in the binary tree. The next N‑1 lines each contain three space‑separated values: pi (parent node value), ci (child node value) and Ri ('L' or 'R'), indicating whether ci is the left or right child of pi.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single line containing "YES" if the given binary tree satisfies all BST properties, otherwise print "NO".

solution.approach: The tree is built using a hash map that stores each value as a node object. While reading the edges we also keep a set of all child values; the node whose value never appears as a child is the root. Once the tree is constructed we perform a depth‑first traversal keeping the allowed value range for each node (min, max). A node is valid iff its value lies strictly between min and max and both sub‑trees are valid with updated ranges. This classic min‑max method works in O(N) time per test case and uses O(N) extra memory for the nodes and auxiliary structures.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(total N) for all test cases combined.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) per test case for storing the nodes and auxiliary hash maps.
